item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is great. And it's got a lot of differences from Pontypool. It's not violent. It also offers a lot of explanation that I felt the movie was lacking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why the burning? "SCP" does not stand for "Destroy, Destroy, and Destroy."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In addition to the other references, this also reminds me of Squanch from Rick and Morty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must congratulate the author of this skip.

I typically have nightmares about being unable to communicate with others instead of nightmares about falling like other people do.

This happens to be my personal bugbear, and was well written enough to give me the heebie-jeebies, the willies, the [REDACTED].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hodor? Hodor, hodor!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really similar to 546.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could provide for a pretty cool tale. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait a second, if they tested a mass infected person (Only uses the word in speech) and know that's what they were saying, wouldn't it spread to that observer as well? Just a little kink in it I'd say. Also, couldn't this take over the world or something? Maybe make a reference to that already happening (may push it) If you can pull it off properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The infected seem to be able to communicate with each other without a problem. If something like this breached containment, we might not be able to tell that anything had changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. I can just imagine every human on Earth repeating the phrase, non-stop, for every sentence of every conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best kajigger ever!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the final stages of infection, every utterance of SCP-1098-1 becomes a string of variously-inflected instances of SCP-1098. These effects also appear in written communication.

This reminds me of the movie 'Being John Malkovich,' When John went through the door and entered his own head:

(In a classy restaurant.)

The waiter, John Malkovich: "Malkovich, malkovich malkovich?"

The lady ordering, John Malkovich: (thoughtful pause) "Malkovich."

The waiter: "Malkovich! Malcovich malcovich."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Malkovich Malkovich? Malkovich. Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich, Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich Malkovich. Malkovich!

…seriously, though, I like this. I do have one complaint. though; the description states that it's safe to hear the word "out of context", but that hearing it "in context" causes the infection. I don't, however, see it defined anywhere what the "context" is.

Also, what would happen if a deaf-mute who could read lips saw someone speak the word?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of fishmonger's SCPs. it was a sylabtoog that eventually bleedponks words you know until your whole vocabulary is loncard with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of 566, but I'm prejudiced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I got hung up on the specific words that are first replaced by the SCP. Even a poor English speaker would use 'something' or 'thing' to replace a word they weren't sure of. So yeah, even a first-year college-level ESL learner would be at risk of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Totally this.
I'm decent at writing , but well, i speak quite quickly, have a thick accent, and besides, i'm not that good at speaking even in my own language.
Written communication FTW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the reason I prefer to communicate in written form. As large as my English vocabulary is, the chances of someone misunderstanding my meaning completely is terrifyingly high when trying to TALK to someone. At least with text I can rewrite something until it actually says what I mean and THEN let people read it.

Upvoting for this reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Smurfy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What was it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the old 908

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find that the beauty of this psychohazard is that you don't need to have much mastery of the English language, as victims of prolonged infection begin to substitute more words with the infectious agent. Also, memetic hazards can mutate over a given period of time, much like real-life viruses, so the chance of it mutating to be able to use another language as a vector is possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a "1088".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except "marklar" is always a noun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this idea because not being able to make yourself understood is actually extremely distressing at the best of times and often fucking terrifying.
Trust me on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great! (Sadly, I had a similar idea that I guess I won't be using now!)

Question: effects on non-English speakers. Does that include non-native speakers? How much English proficiency does one need to be at risk of using this word?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved Pontypool. It should be required viewing in the "understanding memetics" section.

However, this isn't quite the same thing. And by "not quite", I mean "not by a long shot". The Pontypool cognitohazard was distinct from the language snippets it infected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's very similar to that word-virus in the movie Pontypool.
Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed article title in index from "Contagious Thingamajig" to "Virulent Word"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's due to the fact it's 5AM, but I do like this one.
Not all memetic contagions are deadly , as Spikebrennan says.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More changes:

  • clarified that the SCP is a █-syllable english-like word
  • provided examples of "placeholder names" to make usage of the SCP clearer.
  • changed some phrasing of emotional reactions of SCP-1098-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concept isn't bad at all- this is exactly how a mild memetic threat would work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could have come up with something better than placeholder name. Perhaps "An English Phrase" since its written in english?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was interested by All written instances of SCP-1098 are to be burned, painted over, or otherwise obliterated.

The rest reminds me too much of 1) oh man this TV show episode where 2) Bees, and 3) toaster. Withholding a downvote because I like the idea of language-meaning manipulation, but it needs some conceptual overhauling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just reminded me of that South Park episode where the aliens call all objects or people 'marklar'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I smurfing agree

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Oh, so that's what it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This smurfing idea has potential, but the problem is that as soon as I smurfed it the only smurf I could smurf about were smurfs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I moved the paragraph you specified and juggled the sentence order to make it a better conclusion for the article. I've also added an example quote to make it clearer what i'm talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mispost, ignore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would suggest that the second and third paragraphs be switched, as you use 1098-1 before explaining what it is.

I would also word this differently, right now it reads like you weren't sure what to call it, and so inserted a placeholder.

I get what you're trying to do, and it shows promise, but for now, novote.
